Welcome back. Today we're going to be discussing writing your initial offer letter. This is a very exciting time. For now, just know that you don't only send an offer. To show real interest and to be taken seriously, you also have to show financial ability to purchase the business. To begin, you'll want to download one of the two included exclusive letters of intent in the resources section of this module. One is designed for purchasing equity in the business and the other is for purchasing assets. We recommend that you do an asset purchase to best protect yourself in an acquisition process. When you open the document, you'll see it's what we call a boilerplate contract. It's missing a good amount of information that you'll need to fill in for your deal. We're not your lawyers, so we're only giving you examples. We've made this an easy step-by-step process by highlighting all the areas that you'll be able to communicate with your professionals about. There are several very important sections in the letter that you should be familiar with and ensure are included and agreed to by the seller. First, for the benefit of the seller and because you're required to do so, you must disclose how you intend to finance the business. And as I already said, you'll have to also include further documentation on financing. Next, you will notice that this letter is contingent on a due diligence review. This protects you, the buyer. Immediately upon both parties agreeing to this document, you as the buyer will jump into an extensive due diligence process where you can look at all aspects of the business. You'll actually have the right to do this. If you find something concerning, this section allows you to back out of the deal if you're surprised by any hidden expectations or responsibilities. Next, while you're doing your due diligence and before closing, the business owner agrees to conduct business in the ordinary course of business.

## [03:01 - 06:03]

This is legal language saying that they must continue to run the business in good standing while negotiations and due diligence are being carried out. This protects you from the owner allowing the business to decline during this process. And finally, you'll want to include exclusive negotiation rights. This gives you the exclusive rights to negotiate with the seller. It prevents them from taking your offer, going out into the marketplace and trying to solicit other offers that are higher than yours. This gives exclusive rights to negotiate with the sellers. It prevents them from actually taking your offer, going out to other potential bidders and trying to create a bidding war. In summary, this document is to show your serious intent to purchase the business, disclose any financial and fundraising that you'll need, as well as protect you as the buyer. Next, you'll get into the business of actually negotiating each individual key term in the purchase agreement. And I will tell you, the more you can cover that off in the letter of intent, the less you have to cover it off in the actual purchase agreement. This is essentially an executive summary of the deal that includes the purchase details, but also information such as working capital that will be left in the business. The adjustments that you'll need against working capital that need to be made in order for you to buy the business. The timeframe for closing the business, whether the high level issues of warranties, covenants, all the employee agreements, everything we've talked about previous to this module, you'll want this covered off in the document so you have time to assess it, test it, and renegotiate those specific elements if necessary. The last thing I'd like to cover in this lesson is the proper manner of sending the initial letter of intent. Always send this as a letter, not as an email or text. During that holding pattern, move on to the investigation of other businesses to purchase. Never get tunnel vision and only work on one deal.
